summary.overview: Indiana is experiencing a historic energy affordability crisis, with average residential electric bills surging 17.5% statewide in a single year (2024–2025) — the largest year-over-year jump in at least two decades — adding roughly $28/month to household costs. Indiana's five largest investor-owned utilities (Duke Energy Indiana, AES Indiana, NIPSCO, CenterPoint Energy, and Indiana Michigan Power) have all recently received or are seeking major rate increases, driving record levels of consumer complaints to the IURC. Governor Braun responded by appointing a new, ratepayer-focused IURC majority and Utility Consumer Counselor, and signed landmark legislation (HEA 1002) in February 2026 that introduces performance-based ratemaking to tie utility returns to affordability benchmarks — though advocates warn the reforms do not go far enough for average ratepayers.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Low-income ratepayers and households receiving LIHEAP/EAP
messaging_recommendations.message: You are not alone — Indiana's energy assistance programs (EAP/LIHEAP) offer moratorium protection from disconnection through March 15 and up to $625 in heating benefits; apply before April 20 each year and know your rights. Connect local, personal stories of energy insecurity to statewide data showing bills have risen more than 17% in one year to build public pressure on regulators and legislators.

Performance-based ratemaking under HEA 1002 is a meaningful first step, but structural protections — including robust low-income rate discounts, hard caps on rate increases, and strong enforcement of the moratorium — are needed to ensure accountability translates to real bill relief for the most vulnerable Hoosiers.

Framing energy affordability as a 'kitchen-table' crisis — with average Hoosier households paying $28/month more than last year, and NIPSCO customers facing a 90%+ bill increase since 2016 — makes utility policy tangible and urgent, counteracting utility industry messaging that portrays rate increases as necessary for reliability and investment.

messaging_recommendations.audience: Utility company executives and regulatory parties
messaging_recommendations.message: Hoosiers are watching and the regulatory landscape has changed: the IURC is actively investigating affordability, the OUCC is recommending rate reductions rather than increases, and new state law requires utilities to earn returns through demonstrated performance — making proactive low-income programs and billing transparency not just good public relations but regulatory necessity.

rate_cases.description: Duke Energy Indiana requested a two-phase increase of approximately $491.5 million in annual revenues. The IURC approved a reduced increase of approximately $295.7 million on January 29, 2025, implementing roughly 8% in February 2025 and approximately 3% in early 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately 11% overall average bill increase (~$19.16/month for residential 1,000 kWh customer)

rate_cases.description: AES Indiana filed a regulatory rate review on June 3, 2025, requesting an increase of approximately $192.9 million in annual revenues, proposing a two-phase implementation of roughly 7.5% in Q2 2026 and 6% in January 2027. The OUCC recommended a rate reduction, not an increase; a partial settlement among some parties was filed October 2025 but opposed by the OUCC and CAC. The IURC deadline for a final order is June 24, 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Requested: ~13.5% over two phases (~$21/month); OUCC recommends a $21.2 million rate reduction instead

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO filed a major electric base rate case in September 2024 seeking approximately $368.7 million in additional revenues. A settlement agreement was approved by the IURC on June 26, 2025, resulting in an overall average residential increase of approximately 16.75% ($23/month), phased in from July 2025 through Q1 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately 16.75% (~$23/month for residential customer using 672 kWh/month)

rate_cases.description: NIPSCO Gas filed a natural gas base rate case; a settlement agreement was approved by the IURC in July 2024, resulting in an increase of $120.9 million — $41 million less than NIPSCO's initial request. The case addressed infrastructure costs for 32 counties of gas service territory.
rate_cases.rate_change: $120.9 million annual revenue increase (reduced from initial request)

rate_cases.description: CenterPoint Energy filed for an electric base rate increase of $118.8 million (16.02%) in December 2023. The IURC unanimously approved a settlement on February 3, 2025, resulting in approximately a 20% overall bill increase phased in two steps: first in March 2025 and second in March 2026.
rate_cases.rate_change: Approximately 20% overall (~$80 million approved increase, phased through March 2026)

rate_cases.description: Indiana Michigan Power's most recent base rate case resulted in a settlement-based increase approved in May 2024. The IURC approved $61.8 million in additional revenues over two phases; I&M's current rates have been in place since May 2024 and the utility's authorized trackers account for approximately 20.2% of a residential bill.
rate_cases.rate_change: $61.8 million annual revenue increase (settlement; 43% less than originally requested)

articles.headline: Hoosiers' electric bills spike 17.5% in one year
articles.summary: A Citizens Action Coalition analysis of Indiana's five investor-owned electric utilities found that average residential bills rose 17.5%, or $28 per month, in the 12 months ending July 1, 2025 — the steepest year-over-year increase in at least 20 years. NIPSCO customers were hit hardest with a $50/month (26.7%) increase, while CenterPoint bills surged $44/month (25%). The report found that AES Indiana bills rose nearly $17/month (12%) and Duke Energy bills were up approximately $26/month (20%).

articles.headline: Indiana governor seeks lower utility rates: 'We can't take it anymore.'
articles.summary: Indiana Gov. Mike Braun publicly pressed for lower utility rates and directed newly appointed Utility Consumer Counselor Abby Gray to evaluate utility profits and identify cost-saving measures. The governor cited the 17.5% average bill surge — the most in 20 years — and expressed concern about the cumulative impact of data-center-driven energy demand on residential ratepayers. Braun's statement came days before he appointed a new OUCC leader and signaled a shift in the state's regulatory posture.

articles.headline: State's ratepayer advocate recommends denial of AES' $200M proposed increase
articles.summary: Newly appointed Utility Consumer Counselor Abby Gray and 11 OUCC witnesses filed testimony recommending the IURC deny AES Indiana's $192.9 million rate increase request — instead recommending a $21.2 million rate reduction. Gray noted AES filed its request less than 14 months after the previous rate case was decided. The filing included more than 6,800 written consumer comments opposing the rate hike, described as unprecedented in volume.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators call in 'big five' energy providers for affordability investigation
articles.summary: The IURC announced it would hold a public investigative inquiry on energy affordability, calling in all five of Indiana's largest investor-owned utilities — AES Indiana, Duke Energy Indiana, NIPSCO, CenterPoint Energy, and Indiana Michigan Power — to answer questions about rising bills and billing transparency. The inquiry was announced by new IURC Chair Andy Zay in response to a record number of consumer complaints about high utility bills. This represented one of the first formal accountability moves under the Braun-appointed IURC majority.

articles.headline: 'A real short-term crisis here': Regulators press Indiana utilities on rising energy costs
articles.summary: The IURC conducted a daylong public hearing at which Chair Andy Zay told AES Indiana 'we have a real short-term crisis here' and pressed all five utilities on their billing transparency and affordability measures. Utilities largely attributed high bills to customer usage and infrastructure investment costs, drawing criticism from legislators and consumer advocates present at the hearing. The IURC announced it would proceed to a second phase of the inquiry, consisting of 10 community listening sessions across Indiana.

articles.headline: 'Big five' Indiana utilities defend rates in regulator questioning
articles.summary: All five major Indiana utilities appeared before the IURC to defend their rates, with NIPSCO drawing particular scrutiny for having the highest residential bills in the state ($234/month for 1,000 kWh) and the most complaints — with complaints in 2026 already matching all of 2025's total by March. Commissioner Anthony Swinger highlighted NIPSCO's disproportionate share of consumer affairs complaints, while CenterPoint's bills were cited at $221/month — a 25% increase. AES Indiana President Brandi Davis-Handy argued that the company's bills have largely tracked inflation over time.

articles.headline: NIPSCO overcharged 3,500 natural gas customers, newly unsealed records show
articles.summary: Newly unsealed IURC records revealed that NIPSCO overcharged more than 3,500 natural gas customers double their actual usage after installing new smart meters, a problem discovered in October 2024 that was not disclosed to state regulators for nearly a year. NIPSCO proposed refunding affected customers for only 12 months of overcharges per IURC rules, a position that Citizens Action Coalition called a violation of public trust. The disclosure came amid the IURC's ongoing affordability inquiry, adding to public scrutiny of the utility.

articles.headline: Gov. Braun says new law will protect Indiana utility customers from rising bills
articles.summary: Governor Mike Braun signed House Enrolled Act 1002 (HEA 1002) into law at a ceremonial event at the Statehouse, describing the legislation as landmark reform that overhauls how Indiana regulates investor-owned utilities. The law introduces performance-based ratemaking, requiring utilities to meet measurable affordability and reliability benchmarks before earning their authorized return on equity. It also mandates more consistent and predictable billing, and requires utilities to offer low-income programs; Citizens Action Coalition said the law helps low-income Hoosiers but may not deliver meaningful relief for average ratepayers.

articles.headline: Indiana utility regulators hold public meeting on rising bills
articles.summary: The IURC held a public listening session in New Haven as part of the second phase of its investigative inquiry into rising utility costs, allowing Indiana residents to share firsthand accounts of how high energy bills have affected their lives. The session was part of a 10-stop community listening tour held March 26 through April 2026. Regulators indicated they are gathering evidence of customer harm — including copies of utility bills — to develop concrete action steps and potential regulatory interventions.

articles.headline: Michiana representative calls for NIPSCO rate review amid dramatic utility bill increases
articles.summary: State Rep. Jim Pressel (R-Rolling Prairie) sent a formal letter to IURC Chair Andy Zay calling for an investigation into NIPSCO's rising residential utility rates, citing a flood of constituent complaints and IURC survey data showing NIPSCO customers using 1,000 kWh in July 2025 paid over 90% more than they did in 2016. Pressel co-authored HB 1002, the utility affordability reform bill, and requested the IURC examine NIPSCO's investments, billing transparency, and short-term options for rate stability. His letter was co-signed by 15 other northern Indiana legislators.

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: "We have a real short-term crisis here. The reality is, on Main Street, there are people that simply can't afford to pay these bills, whether they're higher or consistent or not." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: WFYI / Indiana Public Radio news report on IURC hearing

stakeholders.name: Andy Zay
stakeholders.title: Chairman
stakeholders.quote: "In my short time as chairman of the IURC, my fellow commissioners and I have heard from thousands of utility customers from all over Indiana that they are struggling to pay their energy bills." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: IURC press release announcing community listening sessions

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor (Indiana's 25th)
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "AES Indiana has not demonstrated sufficient evidence for an increase in this rate case, which it filed less than 14 months after receiving Commission approval in its last rate case. My team is recommending additional reductions that would reduce the utility's current rates and help lessen the burden on ratepayers." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: OUCC press release / IURC testimony filing in Cause No. 46258

stakeholders.name: Abby Gray
stakeholders.title: Utility Consumer Counselor
stakeholders.organization: Indiana Office of Utility Consumer Counselor (OUCC)
stakeholders.quote: "The settlement agreement filed by AES and other parties shows the utility's initial request was inflated and unnecessary. The OUCC stands by its initial recommendations for a reduction from current rates and will oppose the utility's settlement agreement." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle reporting on AES Indiana settlement opposition

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "It is a shame that it had to get to this point before they took action. It is long overdue. Ratepayers from Evansville to Gary have been organizing over outrageously high bills. Finally, someone is listening." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Axios Indianapolis report on IURC affordability investigation announcement

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "These illegal orders rob CenterPoint and NIPSCO ratepayers of the savings promised them from the closure of these antiquated and climate wrecking coal units." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Earthjustice press release on lawsuit challenging DOE emergency coal plant orders

stakeholders.name: Kerwin Olson
stakeholders.title: Executive Director
stakeholders.quote: "The IURC regulatory commission should absolutely have all the authority in the world to keep these monopoly utilities in check." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: WNDU news report on NIPSCO customer advocacy

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: "Affordability is at a crisis point when it comes to our electric utility bills. For example, NIPSCO customers in northwest Indiana are seeing a nearly $50 a month increase." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: WTHR / Indiana Capital Chronicle reporting on CAC bill analysis

stakeholders.name: Ben Inskeep
stakeholders.title: Program Director
stakeholders.quote: "Adding to the strain and frustration, AES Indiana has implemented multiple large rate increases in recent years, amplifying a growing affordability crisis. And now, AES Indiana is proposing a massive $30 per month residential rate increase without any meaningful affordability protection." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: CAC press release / IURC testimony filing in AES Indiana rate case

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: "Utility rates are too high, and my actions to stand up for ratepayers on appointments to the regulatory commission, bring new power to the grid, and overhaul how utilities are regulated to lower Hoosiers' bills is starting to pay off." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Governor's press release on energy affordability strategy results

stakeholders.name: Mike Braun
stakeholders.title: Governor
stakeholders.quote: "On Tuesday, utilities defended their rate increases in front of the IURC. I want to strongly praise Chairman Zay and the entire Commission for taking a hard stand and demanding real accountability. They asked the tough questions and made it clear that automatic pass-throughs and unchecked hikes are no longer acceptable." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Governor's official statement following March 24, 2026 IURC affordability inquiry

stakeholders.name: Jim Pressel
stakeholders.title: State Representative (R-Rolling Prairie)
stakeholders.quote: "I was a little disappointed frankly with the responses from NIPSCO. Didn't seem like we were getting answers to the questions that the commissioners were asking. I was really disappointed that we didn't hear something on how can we lower rates." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: WNDU news report on IURC utility affordability hearing

stakeholders.name: Brandi Davis-Handy
stakeholders.title: President
stakeholders.quote: "For over a decade, AES Indiana has consistently ranked among the lowest residential rates in the state, and that's not by chance. Despite rising costs, we have been disciplined in our planning through measures like our operations and maintenance costs holding flat for the past five years." (exact quote)
stakeholders.source: Indiana Capital Chronicle reporting on AES Indiana settlement rebuttal
